items.statement: The quotient topology is the unique topology on $A$ such that for every space $Y$ and every map $f\colon A\to Y$: $f$ is continuous iff $f\circ\pi\colon X\to Y$ is continuous.
items.intuition: A clean test: a map \emph{out of} a quotient is continuous exactly when its composite with $\pi$ is. This characterises the quotient topology completely.
items.label: Universal property of the quotient
items.gloss: A clean test: a map \emph{out of} a quotient is continuous exactly when its composite with $\pi$ is. This characterises the quotient topology completely.
items.example: Lets you define continuous maps on glued spaces by defining them on $X$ in a $\sim$-compatible way.
items.proof: We follow the arguments for the proofs of Theorem 5.6 and Theorem 5.13 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the quotient topology $\mathcal{T} \pi$ has the universal property that for every topological space $Y$ and every map $f: A \to Y, f$ is continuous if and only if $f \circ\pi: X \to Y$ is continuous. Let $A$ be given the quotient topology induced by $\pi$, and assume that $f: A \to Y$ is continuous. By definition of the quotient topology $\pi^{-1} (U )$ is open in $X$ if and only if $U$ is open in $A$. Hence, $\pi$ is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 $f \circ\pi: X \to Y$ is continuous. Now assume that $f \circ\pi: X \to Y$ is continuous. Let $V$ be an open set in $Y$. Since $\pi$ is a quotient map and $(f \circ\pi)^{-1} (V ) = \pi^{-1} (f^{-1} (V ))$ is open in $X$ by assumption of continuity of $f \circ\pi$, it follows that $f^{-1} (V )$ is open in $A$. Thus $f$ is continuous. Let $\mathcal{T} '$ be a topology on $A$ with the universal property that for every topological space $Y$ and every map $f: A \to Y, f$ is continuous if and only if $f \circ\pi: X \to Y$ is continuous. We must show that $\mathcal{T} ' = \mathcal{T} \pi$. Let $\mathcal{T}$ be the topology on $X$, and let $A$ be given the topology $\mathcal{T} '$. First let $Y = A$ with the quotient topology induced by $f \circ\pi$. Then for $f =$ id $: (A, \mathcal{T} ' ) \to (A, \mathcal{T} \pi )$, we have id $\circ\pi = \pi: (X, \mathcal{T} ) \to (A, \mathcal{T} \pi )$ which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

items.statement: If $h\colon X\to Y$ is a homeomorphism with $h(x_0)=y_0$, then $h_*\colon\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ is an isomorphism.
items.intuition: Homeomorphic spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups — so different $\pi_1$ proves spaces are not homeomorphic.
items.label: π₁ is a topological invariant
items.gloss: Homeomorphic spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups — so different $\pi_1$ proves spaces are not homeomorphic.
items.example: Since $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)=0$ but $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$, the plane and the circle are not homeomorphic.
items.proof: We must show that $h*$ is bijective. Let $k: Y \to X$ be the inverse homemorphism of $h$ such that $k(y_0 ) = x_0$. Then, by Theorem 7.24, $k* \circ h* = (k\circ h)* =$ (id$X )*$, and $h* \circ k* = (h\circ k)* =$ (id$Y )*$. Hence, $k* = (h* )^{-1}$.

items.statement: For based spaces $(X,x_0),(Y,y_0)$: $\pi_1(X\times Y,(x_0,y_0))\cong\pi_1(X,x_0)\times\pi_1(Y,y_0)$.
items.intuition: The fundamental group of a product is the direct product of the fundamental groups — loops split into their coordinate loops.
items.label: π₁ of a product
items.gloss: The fundamental group of a product is the direct product of the fundamental groups — loops split into their coordinate loops.
items.example: $\pi_1(T^2)=\pi_1(S^1\times S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$.
items.proof: Let pr 1 $: X \times Y \to X$ and pr 2 $: X \times Y \to Y$ be the projection maps onto the first and second factor, respectively. Define the map $\phi: \pi_1 (X \times Y, (x_0, y_0 )) \to \pi_1 (X, x_0 ) \times \pi_1 (Y, y_0 )$ by ( ) ( ) $\phi([h]) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h]) =$ [pr 1 $\circ h],$ [pr 2 $\circ h]$. Note that $\pi_1 (X, x_0 ) \times \pi_1 (Y, y_o )$ is a group with binary operation given by $([f ], [g]) * ([f ' ], [g ' ]) = ([f ] * [f ' ], [g] * [g ' ])$ for $[f ], [f ' ] \in \pi_1 (X, x_0 )$ and $[g], [g ' ] \in \pi_1 (Y, y_0 )$. We will show that $\phi$ is an isomorphism. It is a homomorphism since (pr 1 )$*$ and (pr 2 )$*$ are homomorphisms: ( ) $\phi([h] * [k]) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h] * [k]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h] * [k]) ( ) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h]) *$ (pr 1 )$* ([k]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h]) *$ (pr 2 )$* ([k]) ( ) ( ) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h]) *$ (pr 1 )$* ([k]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([k]) = \phi([h]) * \phi([k])$ for $[h], [k] \in \pi_1 (X \times Y, (x_0, y_0 ))$. Let $f: I \to X$ be a loop in $X$ based at $x_0$, and let $g: I \to Y$ be a loop in $Y$ based at $y_0$. Define the map $h: I \to X \times Y$ by $h(s) = (f (s), g(s))$. Then $h$ is a loop in $X \times Y$ based at $(x_0, y_0 )$, and ( ) ( ) $\phi([h]) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h]) =$ [pr 1 $\circ h],$ [pr 2 $\circ h] = ([f ], [g])$. Hence, $\phi$ is surjective. To show that $\phi$ is injective, assume that $\phi([h]) = \phi([k])$ for $[h], [k] \in \pi_1 (X \times Y, (x_0, y_0 ))$. Then there is a path homotopy $H_1: I \times I \to X$ from pr 1 $\circ h$ to pr 1 $\circ k$ and a path homotopy $H_2: I \times I \to Y$ from pr 2 $\circ h$ to pr 2 $\circ k$. Define the map $H: I \times I \to X \times Y$ by ( ) $H(s, t) = H_1 (s, t), H_2 (s, t)$. Then $H$ is a path homotopy from $h =$ (pr 1 $\circ h$, pr 2 $\circ h)$ to $k =$ (pr 1 $\circ k$, pr 2 $\circ k)$. Hence, $[h] = [k]$, and so, $\phi$ is injective. Thus $\phi$ is an isomorphism.

items.statement: For any $s_0\in S^1$, the lifting correspondence $\Phi\colon\pi_1(S^1,s_0)\to\mathbb{Z}$ is a group isomorphism.
items.intuition: The fundamental group of the circle is the integers, with the integer counting how many times a loop winds around. The first non-trivial $\pi_1$ computation.
items.label: π₁(S¹) ≅ ℤ
items.gloss: The fundamental group of the circle is the integers, with the integer counting how many times a loop winds around. The first non-trivial $\pi_1$ computation.
items.example: It underlies the $2$-dimensional Brouwer theorem and the fundamental theorem of algebra.
items.proof: Since $S^{1}$ is path-connected, it follows by Theorem 7.19 that we only need to compute $\pi_1 (S^{1}, s_0 )$ for one basepoint (and hence, all) $s_0 \in S^{1}$. By Theorem 8.6, we know that the map $p: \mathbb{R} \to S^{1}$ given by $p(t) =$ (cos(2$\pi t)$, sin(2$\pi t))$ is a covering map. Furthermore, since $\mathbb{R}$ is simply connected, it follows by Theorem 8.15 that the lifting correspondence $\Phi: \pi_1 (S^{1}, s_0 ) \to p^{-1} (s_0 )$ is a bijection. Let $e_0 = 0$ such that $p(e_0 ) = p(0) = (1, 0) = s_0$. Clearly, $p^{-1} (s_0 ) = \mathbb{Z}$. We want to show that $\Phi$ is a homomorphism. In other words, if $[f ], [g] \in \pi_1 (S^{1}, s_0 )$ we want to show that $\Phi([f ] * [g]) = \Phi([f ]) + \Phi([g])$. Assume that $f: I \to S^{1}$ and $g: I \to S^{1}$ are two loops in $S^{1}$ based at $s_0$. By Theorem 8.12 there are unique lifts $\tilde{f}$ and $\tilde{g}$ of $f$ and $g$, respectively, such that $\tilde{f}(0) = \tilde{g}(0) = 0$. Let $\tilde{f}(1) = x$ and $\tilde{g}(1) = y$ such that, by definition of $\Phi$, we have $\Phi([f ]) = x$ and $\Phi([g]) = y$. We will show that $\Phi([f * g]) = x + y$. To compute $\Phi([f * g])$ we must lift $f * g$ to a path $\tilde{f} * g$ in $\mathbb{R}$ such that $(\tilde{f} * g)(0) = 0$. Let $\tilde{h}: I \to \mathbb{R}$ be a path in $\mathbb{R}$ given by $\tilde{h}(s) = \tilde{f}(1) + \tilde{g}(s) = x + \tilde{g}(s)$. Note that $\tilde{h}$ is a lift of $g$ starting at $x$. Let $\tilde{f} * \tilde{h}: I \to \mathbb{R}$ be a path in $\mathbb{R}$ given by { $\tilde{f}(2s) 0 \leq s \leq 1/2 (\tilde{f} * \tilde{h})(s) = \tilde{h}(2s^{-1}) 1/2 \leq s \leq 1$.

items.label: arbitrary union of open sets is open
items.statement: In a topological space, any union of open sets is open.
items.formal_statement: $$\{U_i\}_{i\in I}\subseteq\tau_X\Longrightarrow\bigcup_{i\in I}U_i\in\tau_X.$$
items.intuition: Open sets may be glued together by arbitrary unions without leaving the topology.

concept_map.description: Concept-level map of spaces, definitions, examples, and motivating relations.
concept_map.example_traversal: topological_space -> defined_by -> topology; compactness -> has_example -> cantor_set; quotient_space_examples -> motivates -> quotient_topology

definition_dependency_graph.description: Definitions needed to state concepts, structures, and theorems.
definition_dependency_graph.example_traversal: tychonoff_theorem -> requires -> product_space, product_topology, compactness, open_set

theorem_dependency_graph.description: Logical dependencies among results and assumptions, separated from definitional needs.
theorem_dependency_graph.example_traversal: tychonoff_theorem -> uses -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> uses -> zorns_lemma; tychonoff_theorem -> assumes -> axiom_of_choice

proof_graph.description: Proofs, proof steps, proof methods, and results used by particular proofs.

inheritance_tree.description: Tree-like or DAG-like inheritance view of mathematical structures; multiple inheritance is retained.
inheritance_tree.example_traversal: incoming subtype_of from topological_space, then from compact_space and hausdorff_space to compact_hausdorff_space

construction_graph.description: How spaces and topologies are constructed from prior data.
construction_graph.example_traversal: product_topology -> constructed_from -> subbasis; product_topology -> induces -> product_space

prerequisite_map.description: Pedagogical order; useful for learning but not always logical necessity.
prerequisite_map.example_traversal: set -> topology -> open_set -> compactness -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> tychonoff_theorem

application_map.description: Where theorems, methods, and constructions are applied or illustrated.

traversal_primitives.outgoing: Follow edges whose source is the current node.
traversal_primitives.incoming: Follow edges whose target is the current node.
traversal_primitives.both: Follow incoming and outgoing edges while preserving direction in returned paths.
traversal_primitives.multigraph_rule: Do not collapse parallel edges; edge type and dependency_class determine meaning.

dependency_semantics.definitions_needed_to_state: requires edges with dependency_class definitional_dependency or notation_dependency
dependency_semantics.proof_tools_used: uses edges from theorem, proof, or proof_step to proof_method, lemma, theorem, proposition, or axiom

example_queries.example: From tychonoff_theorem return product_space, product_topology, compactness, open_set, open_cover_notation, alexander_subbase_theorem, zorns_lemma, and axiom_of_choice grouped by dependency_class.
From urysohn_metrization_theorem follow requires to regularity, hausdorff_property, second_countability, and metric_topology.
From heine_borel_theorem keep targets of kind proof_method: open_cover_argument, finite_subcover_argument.
From tychonoff_theorem follow assumes to axiom_of_choice; from urysohn_metrization_theorem follow assumes to regularity_assumption, hausdorff_assumption, and second_countability_assumption.
From compactness follow has_example to cantor_set and circle; also inspect incoming has_property from examples for additional satisfying instances.
From hausdorff_assumption use incoming shows_necessity_of to get non_hausdorff_spaces_as_counterexamples, then inspect compact_subsets_of_hausdorff_are_closed -> has_counterexample.
From topological_space follow incoming subtype_of; compact_hausdorff_space appears under both compact_space and hausdorff_space.

One path is set -> topology -> open_set -> compactness -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> tychonoff_theorem via prerequisite_for; enrich with tychonoff_theorem -> requires -> product_topology and tychonoff_theorem -> assumes -> axiom_of_choice.
